preamble.summary: A psalm of Solomon. Unless the LORD builds the house and guards the city, all human labor is futile. God gives to his beloved even in sleep. The psalm then shifts to children: sons are an inheritance from the LORD, like arrows in a warrior's hand. The man whose quiver is full of them will not be put to shame when he confronts his opponents at the city gate.
preamble.remarkable: This is the only Song of Ascents attributed to Solomon, and the attribution is deeply fitting. Solomon built the temple — the house of the LORD — yet the psalm opens by declaring that unless the LORD builds the house, the builders labor in vain. If Solomon wrote or inspired this psalm, it is a confession that his greatest achievement depended entirely on God's initiative. The psalm contains one of the most radical statements about human effort in the entire Bible: it is useless (shav) to rise early, stay up late, and eat the bread of anxious toil, because God gives to his beloved in sleep. This is not an endorsement of laziness but a demolition of the illusion of self-sufficiency. The pivot to children in verses 3-5 is not a non sequitur — children are the ultimate example of something humans cannot manufacture by effort alone. They are nachalat YHWH ('an inheritance from the LORD'), a gift, not a product.
preamble.friction: The relationship between the two halves of this psalm has puzzled interpreters. Verses 1-2 address the futility of human effort without God; verses 3-5 celebrate children as God's gift. The connection becomes clear when 'house' (bayit) is understood in its full Hebrew range: bayit means both a physical building and a family/dynasty. Solomon's bayit was both a temple and a royal house. The psalm says God must build both — the structure and the family line. Without God, neither architecture nor offspring accomplishes anything.
preamble.connections: The Solomonic attribution connects directly to 2 Samuel 7, where God tells David that God will build David a 'house' (dynasty), inverting David's plan to build God a house (temple). The futility language (shav, 'in vain') echoes Ecclesiastes, also attributed to Solomon, which repeatedly declares human effort hevel ('vapor, breath'). The arrow imagery for children appears in Isaiah 49:2, where the Servant of the LORD is described as a polished arrow hidden in God's quiver.

verses.text_hebrew: שִׁ֥יר הַֽמַּעֲל֗וֹת לִשְׁלֹמֹ֫ה אִם־יְהוָ֤ה ׀ לֹא־יִבְנֶ֬ה בַ֗יִת שָׁ֤וְא ׀ עָמְל֣וּ בוֹנָ֣יו בּ֑וֹ אִם־יְהוָ֥ה לֹֽא־יִשְׁמׇר־עִ֝֗יר שָׁ֣וְא שָׁקַ֣ד שׁוֹמֵֽר׃
verses.text_kjv: Except the LORD build the house, they labour in vain that build it: except the LORD keep the city, the watchman waketh but in vain.
verses.rendering:
A song of ascents. Of Solomon.
Unless the LORD builds the house,
its builders labor over it in vain.
Unless the LORD guards the city,
the watchman stays awake in vain.
verses.expanded_rendering: The conditional im ('if, unless') structures both lines as absolute conditions: without God's involvement, the outcome is shav ('emptiness, vanity, futility'). The word bayit ('house') carries immense freight in Hebrew — it means a physical structure, a household, a family line, a dynasty. Solomon built the most famous bayit in Israelite history (the temple), yet this psalm attributed to him declares that the building means nothing without God as the true builder. The parallel line extends the principle to civic protection: the shomer ('watchman') who stays awake through the night (shaqad, 'to be wakeful, to keep vigil') accomplishes nothing if the LORD is not the city's true guardian. Human diligence without divine involvement is shav — the same word used in the third commandment ('do not take the name of the LORD your God in vain').
translator_notes: The word shav ('in vain, emptiness') appears twice, framing both human activities — building and guarding — as equally futile without God. shav does not mean 'bad' or 'sinful'; it means 'empty, without substance, accomplishing nothing.' The psalm does not condemn work but reframes its meaning.

The Solomonic attribution (li-Shelomoh) may indicate authorship, dedication, or association with the Solomonic wisdom tradition. Given the psalm's themes of building and God's sovereignty over human achievement, the connection to Solomon the temple-builder is theologically resonant regardless of the attribution's historical basis.

key_terms.semantic_range: house, household, family, dynasty, temple, lineage
key_terms.note: bayit is one of the most important words in the Hebrew Bible. It encompasses physical structures (a house to live in), family units (the 'house of David'), the temple ('the house of the LORD'), and dynastic succession (God's promise to build David a 'house' in 2 Samuel 7). This psalm exploits the full range: unless God builds the house, no house — architectural or familial — stands.

shav describes something that lacks substance or produces no result. It is the word used in the Decalogue's prohibition against taking God's name la-shav ('in vain, for emptiness'). Here it characterizes human effort divorced from divine purpose — not sinful, but substanceless.

verses.text_hebrew: שָׁ֤וְא לָכֶ֨ם ׀ מַשְׁכִּ֪ימֵי ק֡וּם מְאַֽחֲרֵי־שֶׁ֗בֶת אֹ֭כְלֵי לֶ֣חֶם הָעֲצָבִ֑ים כֵּ֤ן יִתֵּ֖ן לִֽידִיד֣וֹ שֵׁנָֽא׃
verses.text_kjv: It is vain for you to rise up early, to sit up late, to eat the bread of sorrows: for so he giveth his beloved sleep.
verses.rendering:
It is useless for you to rise early,
to stay up late,
to eat the bread of anxious toil —
for he gives to his beloved in sleep.
verses.expanded_rendering: Three phrases describe the anxious worker's life: rising before dawn (mashkimei qum), sitting up past dark (me'acharei shevet), and eating lechem ha-atsavim ('the bread of painful toil'). The word atsavim ('toils, pains, anxieties') is from the same root as the curse in Genesis 3:17 — be-itsavon tokhalenah ('in pain you shall eat'). The psalm says that this curse-driven anxiety about provision is unnecessary for those who trust God. The final phrase ken yitten lidido shena ('thus he gives to his beloved sleep') is among the most debated in the Psalter. It can mean 'he gives sleep to his beloved' (God grants restful sleep instead of anxious wakefulness) or 'he gives to his beloved in sleep' (God provides while the beloved sleeps). Both readings converge on the same truth: God's provision does not depend on human striving.
translator_notes: The word yedido ('his beloved') echoes the name Yedidyah ('beloved of the LORD'), the name God gave to Solomon through Nathan the prophet (2 Samuel 12:25). If this psalm is attributed to Solomon, the reference is deeply personal — Solomon himself is the beloved to whom God gives.
The phrase lechem ha-atsavim ('bread of anxious toil') describes food earned through stress and worry — bread that tastes like anxiety because it was produced by it.

verses.text_hebrew: הִנֵּ֤ה נַחֲלַ֣ת יְהוָ֣ה בָּנִ֑ים שָׂ֝כָ֗ר פְּרִ֣י הַבָּֽטֶן׃
verses.text_kjv: Lo, children are an heritage of the LORD: and the fruit of the womb is his reward.
verses.rendering:
Look — children are an inheritance from the LORD;
the fruit of the womb is a reward.
translator_notes: The word nachalat ('inheritance, portion, possession') is covenant language — a nachalah is land or wealth received as a divine gift, not earned by labor. Calling children nachalat YHWH ('an inheritance from the LORD') puts them in the same category as the promised land: something God gives, not something humans produce by their own power. The parallel term sakhar ('reward, wages') reinforces this: children are God's payment, God's gift-in-return.

verses.text_kjv: As arrows are in the hand of a mighty man; so are children of the youth.
verses.rendering:
Like arrows in the hand of a warrior —
so are the children of one's youth.
verses.expanded_rendering: The simile compares children to chitsim ('arrows') in the hand of a gibbor ('warrior, mighty man'). An arrow extends the warrior's reach — it strikes where the hand cannot go. Children born to a young parent (benei hane'urim, 'sons of youth') have time to grow, to be shaped, to be launched into the world while the parent still has strength. The image is dynamic: the parent is the archer, the children are projectiles sent forward into situations the parent cannot personally enter. This is not about control but about purposeful direction — the arrow, once released, flies on its own.
translator_notes: The phrase benei hane'urim ('children of youth') refers to children born when the parent is young and vigorous. The advantage is practical: these children will be adults while the parent is still active, providing support and advocacy (as verse 5 will specify).

verses.text_hebrew: אַשְׁרֵ֤י הַגֶּ֗בֶר אֲשֶׁ֤ר מִלֵּ֬א אֶת־אַשְׁפָּת֗וֹ מֵהֶ֥ם לֹֽא־יֵבֹ֑שׁוּ כִּֽי־יְדַבְּר֖וּ אֶת־א֣וֹיְבִ֖ים בַּשָּֽׁעַר׃
verses.text_kjv: Happy is the man that hath his quiver full of them: they shall not be ashamed, but they shall speak with the enemies in the gate.
verses.rendering:
Happy is the man who has filled
his quiver with them.
They will not be put to shame
when they confront their enemies at the gate.
verses.expanded_rendering: The beatitude ashrei ('happy, blessed, fortunate') introduces the conclusion: the man whose ashpah ('quiver') is full of arrow-children has a decisive advantage. The gate (sha'ar) was the place of public legal proceedings in ancient Israel — disputes were settled, contracts witnessed, and justice administered at the city gate. To 'speak with enemies at the gate' (yedabberu et oyvim basha'ar) means to have advocates and witnesses when you face legal challenge or social confrontation. A man with many sons had a visible, physical support network present at the gate. The psalm's point is not that children are weapons but that they are God-given allies in the public arena of life.
translator_notes: The 'gate' (sha'ar) as a place of justice and public discourse appears throughout the Hebrew Bible (Ruth 4:1-11, Proverbs 31:23, Amos 5:15). Legal disputes, property transactions, and community leadership all centered at the city gate. Having sons present at the gate meant having advocates who could speak on your behalf, witness transactions, and defend family interests.
